problem_summary: 2012 MCM Problem B ("Camping along the Big Long River"): a discrete scheduling/optimization problem. Given a 225-mile river with Y campsites roughly uniformly distributed, and trips that vary by propulsion (oar 4 mph / motor 8 mph) and duration (6–18 days), schedule X trips over a 180-day (6-month) season so that no two trip parties ever occupy the same campsite on the same night, while maximizing the total number of trips (the river's "carrying capacity"). Trips only move downstream, stop one night per campsite, and travel ~8 hr/day. The team reframes "carrying capacity" as the size of the optimal schedule itself, and solves three scenario variants of increasing tourist freedom (fixed routes; free routes; free routes + free types).
models_used: Part 1 — Deterministic 'orbit' (campsite-set) partitioning model: partition the Y campsites into disjoint sets ('orbits'), one (or one group) per trip type, by congruence class mod 25; within each orbit run a 'leader' cycle schedule that fills every campsite every day except unavoidable startup 'forgone loss'

Leader-and-followers periodic cyclic schedule: a fastest-traveling 'leader' advances Q intervals/day; Q copies follow each day so all campsites ahead of the leader are occupied; the pattern repeats as a short cycle (e.g. 2-day cycle at Y=150)

'Skeleton orbit' construction: a minimal subset of campsites (7 sites placed at k + j*(Y+1)/7) that, if traversable by a trip type in one day, guarantees the whole orbit is traversable — used to allocate campsites under given trip-type proportions

Three-tier orbit refinement by river size: one orbit per single type (requires Y >= 306), one orbit per group of 2–3 types (small Y, e.g. Y=150 needs Y >= 149), down to one orbit per propulsion class for very small Y
Part 2 — Stochastic classical-probability route model: with free routes, every feasible downstream route for a trip type is equally likely (uniform/classical probability)

Recursive route-counting (dynamic-programming) model: f(T,x,t) = number of routes reaching campsite x at day t, computed by f(T,x,t)=sum over reachable y of f(T,y,t-1); occupancy probability p(T,x,t)=f(T,x,t)*f(T,Y+1-x,duration)/f(T,Y+1,duration)
Inclusion–exclusion (Jordan formula) to compute q(x,day) = probability that >=2 trips share campsite x on a given day

Greedy construction algorithm with a minimax stopping rule: add trips one at a time; among candidate types pick the one minimizing the max collision probability (minimax); stop adding when that minimax value exceeds the 0.05 small-probability threshold

Part 3 — Monte Carlo / randomized greedy simulation: same probabilistic feasibility test, but the next trip is drawn uniformly at random from all currently-addable trips (models tourists freely choosing type+route); run 8 times to estimate carrying capacity
Small-probability event principle: an event with probability below 0.05 (also tested 0.025, 0.01) is treated as 'not happening', converting a hard no-collision constraint into a probabilistic one
key_techniques: Scenario decomposition by degrees of freedom: three sub-problems ordered by tourist flexibility (fixed routes -> free routes -> free types+routes), with the explicit, defensible prediction that capacity must monotonically decrease across them (2340 -> 500 -> ~493)

Constraint relaxation via 'orbit' partitioning: turning one large coupled scheduling problem into many independent small ones by assigning disjoint campsite sets, so collisions are structurally impossible rather than checked
Number-theoretic site assignment (congruence classes mod 25) to spread each trip type's campsites evenly along the river
Reducing a global feasibility guarantee to a tiny 'skeleton' certificate (7 sites) — prove the hard case and the rest follows
Reformulating the objective: defining 'carrying capacity' = number of trips in the optimal schedule, making the metric directly optimizable
DP recursion to count exponentially many routes in polynomial time, then normalizing counts into occupancy probabilities
Inclusion–exclusion to get exact multi-trip collision probabilities instead of approximating
Greedy + minimax as a constructive heuristic with a principled, interpretable stopping criterion tied to the 0.05 threshold
Casework on the parameter Y with explicit feasibility thresholds (Y>=306, Y>=149, Y>=18) so the answer is general, not just for one Y
Anchoring the free parameter Y=150 to a real river (Grand Canyon, also 225 miles, ~150 sites) for credibility

Three-way sensitivity analysis on the choices that actually mattered: first-trip-type (robust), max hours/day 7–9 (robust), and the 0.05 threshold (deliberately flagged as NON-robust: 0.01->215, 0.025->376, 0.05->500)
why_it_won: "It nailed the judge's core test for Problem B: it treated an open-ended scheduling problem as a family of well-posed optimization problems and produced ACTUAL schedules, not just capacity numbers. The standout is the 'orbit' idea in Part 1 — a genuinely original reformulation that converts a tangled global no-collision constraint into many independent, provably-feasible sub-schedules, with a clean cyclic 'leader' construction and the elegant insight that only unavoidable 'forgone loss' startup days are wasted. Judges reward a novel, simple, near-optimal mechanism, and this is one. The team then showed range and rigor: a deterministic combinatorial model AND a stochastic model with real probability machinery (DP route-counting, inclusion–exclusion, classical-probability occupancy), plus a greedy/minimax construction with an interpretable stopping rule. Crucially they handled the modeler's job of choosing a metric (capacity = schedule size) and the constraint-softening question (when is 'never collide' enforceable?) with the small-probability principle. The three-scenario ladder is the kind of structured, exhaustive coverage judges love, and the sensitivity analysis is honest — it explicitly admits the 0.05 threshold dominates the answer rather than hiding it. Concrete deliverables (full Y=150 calendar schedules, capacity figures of 2340/500/493) and a clear manager's letter round out a complete, judge-friendly package."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-strong and easy for a judge to navigate: Introduction -> Definitions -> formal problem statement -> Assumptions -> three parallel Parts (each with Motivation/justification, Key ideas + a mermaid-style flowchart, Development) -> Sensitivity testing -> Evaluation (per-part strengths/weaknesses) -> Conclusions -> manager's Letter. Standout writing moves: (1) every Part opens with 'Motivation and justification' that ties the abstract model to real river-agency practice (Grand Canyon, Wolf, Missinaibi), grounding modeling choices in reality; (2) a precise Definitions section pins down loaded words ('optimal', 'route', 'orbit', 'forgone loss') before using them, and numbers all 26 trip types in a 2x13 table referenced throughout; (3) worked example schedule tables (Y=425 toy case, then the full Y=150 calendar) make the abstract construction concrete and checkable; (4) flowcharts summarize each model's logic up front; (5) the per-part Strengths/Weaknesses honestly self-critiques each model's realism. The manager's letter restates all three schedules in plain language and frames the trade-off (capacity vs. tourist flexibility) as the manager's decision."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The decisive parameter — the 0.05 small-probability threshold — drives the answer: capacity swings 215/376/500 for 0.01/0.025/0.05. The team flags this but never justifies WHY 0.05 (vs 0.01) is the right risk tolerance, so the headline number 500 rests on an arbitrary choice

The Part 1 capacity of 2340 and the Part 2/3 capacity of ~500 are not directly comparable: 2340 counts the deterministic fixed-route schedule while 500 counts trips launched under a probabilistic collision constraint — different definitions of 'a trip', which obscures the headline comparison

The classical/uniform-probability assumption (all downstream routes equally likely; all trip types equally likely in Part 3) is acknowledged as unrealistic — real tourists cluster behavior — and is never validated against data
The Part 3 results show wildly uneven type usage (oar-25 alone = 40.8%, motor-26 = 16%), and the team admits this lopsidedness 'is not close to reality' but offers no corrective mechanism

One internal inconsistency: trip type 3 (oar, 7 days) is allowed in Part 1 because A3=8.03≈8, but excluded in Parts 2/3 with the rule that daily time must be strictly <8 hr; the 24-vs-25-vs-26 type bookkeeping shifts between parts without a unified rule

A suspicious sensitivity data point: the hours/day table shows capacity 560 at 8 hr but 484 at 7.5 and 483 at 8.5 — a non-monotone spike at the chosen value that the team calls 'low sensitivity' without explaining the anomaly

Assumptions are strong simplifications: campsites exactly uniform, exactly one night per site, downstream-only, fixed average speeds, no weather/rest days, no group-size or campsite-capacity differences
References are weak — four URLs (a Baidu Q&A, two identical rafting-company links, one duplicated) rather than literature or official park data, undercutting the empirical grounding
No explicit optimality bound or comparison to an upper limit: 'almost optimal' and 'forgone loss is negligible' are asserted/illustrated but not proven against a theoretical maximum
reusable_patterns: Scenario ladder by degrees of freedom: split an open scheduling/allocation problem into 2–4 variants ordered by how much choice the actors have, predict the monotone trend in the objective, and let the decision-maker pick the trade-off — a clean way to make an under-specified problem tractable and to demonstrate range

Partition-to-decouple ('orbit') pattern: when a global mutual-exclusion constraint couples everything, partition the resource into disjoint blocks so each block is independently and trivially feasible — turns NP-hard-looking coupling into many easy sub-problems
Skeleton-certificate pattern: prove feasibility on a small worst-case substructure (here 7 sites / the slowest trip type) and argue the full structure inherits it — cheap, rigorous generality

Leader-and-followers periodic cyclic schedule: drive a system with one fastest 'leader' and have others copy/fill behind it, then exploit periodicity so you only specify one short cycle — reusable for any conveyor/pipeline-style scheduling

DP route/path counting + normalization to probabilities: count combinatorially many paths with a forward recursion f(x,t)=Σ f(reachable y, t-1), then turn counts into occupancy/usage probabilities — a general bridge from deterministic enumeration to a stochastic occupancy model
Inclusion–exclusion for exact collision/overlap probabilities instead of independence approximations

Greedy construction with a minimax stopping rule tied to a threshold: add elements that minimize the worst-case violation metric, stop when that metric crosses a stated risk threshold — interpretable, easy to implement, easy to defend
Small-probability constraint softening: convert a hard 'never happens' constraint into 'probability < ε' and treat sub-ε events as impossible — then sensitivity-test ε since it usually dominates

Randomized-greedy Monte Carlo for the 'free choice' variant: replace the optimizing pick with a uniform random feasible pick and run multiple trials to estimate the distribution of outcomes — quick way to model autonomous-agent behavior

Targeted sensitivity analysis that distinguishes robust knobs (first-type, hours/day) from the one knob that controls the answer (the ε threshold), and reports the dangerous one honestly rather than burying it
Anchor a free parameter to a real-world analog (Y=150 from the Grand Canyon's matching 225-mile length) to justify the concrete numeric demonstration
Provide concrete artifact deliverables (a full printable calendar schedule) plus a plain-language decision-maker letter that frames results as a choice, not just a number
